records.ety: [Prob. fr. OE. flecchen to waver, give way, F. flchir, fr. L. flectere to bend; but prob. influenced by E. blench. Cf. Flex.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Flinched (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Flinching.]

defs: To withdraw from any suffering or undertaking, from pain or danger; to fail in doing or preserving; to show signs of yielding or of suffering; to shrink; to wince; as, one of the parties flinched from the combat.
quotes: A child, by a constant course of kindness, may be accustomed to bear very rough usage without flinching or complaining.

defs: To let the foot slip from a ball, when attempting to give a tight croquet.
